interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Civil Rights Activist; San Diego CORE Chairman; Founder of San Diego State Afro-American Studies Program
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Virginia Tech History Department / Civil Rights History Project (Library of Congress and Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture)

main_summary.summary:
Harold K. Brown, known as Hal Brown, recounts his journey from a childhood in segregated York, Pennsylvania to becoming a civil rights leader in San Diego, California. Born in 1934 to Emma Brown, he was one of seven siblings raised in what he calls a 'colored community' where he attended segregated schools through seventh grade and walked past white schools to a Black elementary school, enduring daily racial insults from workers at a chain factory along the route. Brown describes his introduction to racism at age six and the contradictory influences of his white junior high homeroom teacher Cassandra Shea, who introduced him to the concept of college, and a football coach who suggested he could become a dentist. After playing basketball at Penn State on a scholarship, a brief unsuccessful tryout with the St. Louis Browns in Thomasville, Georgia, and two years in the Army (1954-1956) including basic training at Camp Gordon and permanent station at Fort Monmouth, New Jersey, he enrolled at San Diego State, graduating with a major in physical education and a minor in speech pathology.

Unable to find a teaching position because districts would not hire Black coaches, Brown joined the El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee and was approached around 1960 to help form a San Diego chapter of the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE). After waiting a year into his first teaching position, he became chairman of San Diego CORE and later chairman of CORE's Western Region (covering California, Oregon, Washington, and possibly Colorado), and served on the National Advisory Council, where he became friends with James L. Farmer Jr. and Floyd McKissick. The San Diego chapter prioritized employment discrimination, conducting marches, sit-ins, mail-ins, and 'coin-ins' against Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric, ultimately forcing those companies to hire Black workers. Brown was jailed for these demonstrations and called to Sacramento to defend his teaching credential. He notes the chapter was approximately two-thirds white, and that internal struggles with infiltration from the peace movement and accusations involving 'Trotskyites' and communists were more painful than the external fight.

In 1967, Brown married a woman recruited by General Dynamics from Columbus, Ohio as a computer programmer; she could not find housing in San Diego due to discrimination and Brown helped her secure an apartment by threatening to bring a hundred Black friends to the pool. He resigned from CORE around 1967 and went to Lesotho as Deputy Director of the Peace Corps, where he and his wife had to transit through apartheid South Africa. After Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination in April 1968, Brown organized a memorial service at an African Methodist Episcopal Church in Lesotho that King Moshoeshoe II attended (the king was then under house arrest for opposing apartheid). Brown returned to the United States, worked briefly at Floyd McKissick Enterprises in Harlem and at Marine Midland Bank on Wall Street, then returned to San Diego in September 1971 to found San Diego State's Afro-American Studies program as the university's first Black administrator, hiring faculty, establishing a major and minor, and getting its courses accepted for general education credit. He later became associate dean of the College of Business Administration, retiring in 2004 after 26 years. Brown also founded the Black Economic Development Task Force in 1987 and a group he called CLUB (Community Leaders Undoing Biases), and now works in real estate development.

Brown closes by arguing that the unfinished work of the civil rights movement is economic education and wealth building. He cites Tony Brown's KPBS program and laments that contemporary Black leadership including Reverend Jesse L. Jackson Sr. and Reverend Al Sharpton have not centered economics. He acknowledges experiencing what he later recognized as post-traumatic stress after leaving CORE, and connects his own organizing exhaustion to the broader pattern of activist burnout.
key_themes: Northern segregation and Black childhood experience outside the South
Athletics as a limited bridge across racial lines
Employment and housing discrimination in mid-century San Diego
Direct action organizing under CORE (marches, sit-ins, mail-ins, coin-ins)
Internal conflict within civil rights organizations (infiltration, factionalism)
Apartheid encounter and African diaspora identity during Peace Corps service in Lesotho
Black studies institution-building in higher education
Economic self-determination as the unfinished civil rights agenda
main_summary.historical_significance: Brown's testimony documents the under-recorded West Coast civil rights movement and specifically the San Diego CORE chapter's campaigns against Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric, an organizing history Brown himself notes is largely forgotten even among San Diegans of his generation. His account also captures a rare African American Peace Corps administrator's perspective on apartheid-era southern Africa, including the personal connection to Lesotho's King Moshoeshoe II during the period of his house arrest. The interview contributes to the historiography of post-civil-rights-era Black studies institution-building at predominantly white universities, and offers a frank reflection on the unfinished agenda of Black economic development and the long-term psychological costs of sustained movement leadership.

errors: The summary states Brown returned to the U.S. after King's April 1968 assassination; the transcript supports this but the exact sequencing (Brown stayed long enough to organize the memorial service before departing) is condensed and could mislead a reader about timing.

The summary describes Brown's wife as 'a woman recruited by General Dynamics from Columbus, Ohio as a computer programmer.' Transcript says she was a math major who graduated from Alabama State University, was living in Columbus, Ohio, and was recruited to San Diego by General Dynamics as a systems programmer / computer programmer — partial paraphrase risk on biographical specifics.

The summary attributes the wife's recruitment year (1967) to her arrival in San Diego, but the transcript states 'the young lady that I married in 1967, she came to San Diego' — the 1967 marriage year is clear; the recruitment-to-San-Diego arrival timing is less explicit and could be slightly earlier.

The CLUB acronym is rendered 'Community Leaders Undoing Biases' — transcript renders it 'Community Leaders Undoing Biasis' (likely Whisper transcription error for 'Biases'); summary uses the corrected canonical spelling, low risk.
improvements: Trim the sequencing in the Lesotho-to-MLK-memorial paragraph so a reader doesn't conflate Brown's return-trigger with the date of his actual departure.
Add explicit attribution markers ('Brown recalls', 'he says') to soften paraphrase compression on specific dates and biographical attributions.

Consider naming the Cassandra Shea and unnamed football coach details more carefully — the coach is unnamed in the transcript and the summary correctly omits a name, but this is worth flagging in citation audit.

chapters.title: Childhood in Segregated York, Pennsylvania
chapters.summary: Brown introduces himself as Harold K. Brown, known as Hal Brown, born in 1934 in York, Pennsylvania, one of seven siblings of Mrs. Emma Brown. He describes growing up in a predominantly white farm community where Black residents were approximately one percent of the population, in segregated housing in what was then called a 'colored community.' He attended segregated schools through seventh grade, walking approximately five miles past white schools to reach his Black elementary school. He recounts daily racial insults from workers at a chain factory along the route to school, beginning at age six. Brown frames this as his 'introduction to racism in America' and reflects on how the absence of Black professionals in positions of importance shaped a young Black child's sense of inferiority.

improvements: Could quote Brown's exact phrasing about being psychologically conditioned as 'less than the rest of them.'

chapters.title: Teachers, Coaches, and the Absence of Black History
chapters.summary: Brown describes the contradictory influences of his junior high homeroom teacher Cassandra Shea, a white math teacher who introduced him to the concept of college and explained how she had worked through college cleaning houses, and a football coach who suggested he could become a dentist — a profession Brown had never associated with Black people. He notes that there was one Black doctor and no Black dentist in his community. Brown contrasts these encouraging figures with the absence of Black history in his schooling, recalling how Black students would shrink in their seats when slavery images appeared in class and when the music teacher made the class sing 'Old Black Joe.' He says he did not learn Black history until after graduating from college, when he began reading on his own.

chapters.title: Athletics, Penn State, and the Path to San Diego State
chapters.summary: Brown describes playing basketball and baseball from elementary school onward, becoming an all-state basketball player in his senior year and following his brother who had been the first basketball player in their school's history to achieve all-state status. He attended Penn State on a basketball scholarship that paid only tuition, then left to try out for the St. Louis Browns minor league team in Thomasville, Georgia. Unprepared for the conditioning required, he injured his arm playing shortstop and returned home. Two older men from York who had attended San Diego State recruited him; he wrote the San Diego State coach and was advised to attend junior college first to preserve eligibility (advice that turned out to be incorrect). Brown reflects on the importance of guidance and counseling for young people, noting he lacked an adult mentor outside school who could provide that advice.

improvements: Could note the team name 'St. Louis Browns' as the historical pre-Baltimore-Orioles franchise.

chapters.title: Army Service: Camp Gordon and Fort Monmouth
chapters.summary: Brown describes being drafted into the Army between junior college and San Diego State, serving from 1954 to 1956 at the end of the Korean War. He did basic training at Camp Gordon in Georgia, where on-base life was tolerable but off-base he encountered signs reading 'no dogs, Jews or niggers allowed.' He was then permanently stationed at Fort Monmouth, New Jersey, where conditions were better. Brown recounts being assigned to Operation Sagebrush in Louisiana and, as a young enlisted man, going to the post commander to request not to be sent south because of the conditions he knew awaited him; the commander told him to return to his barracks and Brown shipped out anyway. He stayed on the outskirts of town in Louisiana, visiting Shreveport only once.

improvements: Could contextualize Operation Sagebrush as the November 1955 Louisiana Army-Air Force joint exercise.

chapters.title: San Diego State, Student Government, and Constitutional Discrimination
chapters.summary: Brown returns to San Diego State after the Army, majoring in physical education with a minor in speech pathology or speech correction. He estimates approximately 25 Black students in a university enrollment of around 7,000. Brown joins student government and chairs the Constitutions Committee, responsible for reviewing the charters of on-campus fraternities and sororities. Discovering that the charters specified 'white male' or 'white female' membership, he refuses to approve them, denying them on-campus status. He is called in by the dean of students for discussion. Brown describes accumulating anger from constant racial incidents — being called racial slurs walking down the street in San Diego — that prepared him to join the civil rights movement after graduation.

chapters.title: Discovering CORE, Joining the San Diego Chapter
chapters.summary: After graduating, Brown does post-graduate work in speech pathology while seeking a teaching job. He realizes that Black coaches were not hired in high schools. Applying to La Mesa, California, he is told the only way he could be hired is if the district polled all teachers to see if hiring a 'Negro' was acceptable. Brown joins the El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee, an all-white group focused on integrating neighborhoods in La Mesa and El Cajon Valley. Around 1960 a Black acquaintance approaches him about forming a San Diego chapter of CORE (Congress of Racial Equality), which he joins. He simultaneously joins the Afro-American Association, described as a Black nationalist organization focused on educating Black people about their plight and teaching Black history; Brown read Lerone Bennett Jr's work through that group. He delays activism his first teaching year and is named one of San Diego's five outstanding new teachers, then becomes chairman of San Diego CORE around 1961.
keywords: el cajon valley open housing committee

errors: Transcript says Brown joined CORE around 1960 and became chairman around 1961 — both are paraphrased in the summary, low risk.
improvements: Clarify that the 'Afro-American Association' in this context was the local San Diego organization Brown joined; the better-known Donald Warden Afro-American Association in the Bay Area is a different organization with the same name.

chapters.title: Housing Discrimination and Meeting His Wife
chapters.summary: Brown describes the conditions San Diego CORE encountered: 19 segregated schools, employment discrimination at grocery store chains (Black workers could not even be hired as baggers), and severe housing discrimination. Black residents were confined to Logan Heights (now known as Southeast and Logan Heights). Brown recounts meeting his future wife (whom he married in 1967), a math graduate of Alabama State University recruited from Columbus, Ohio by General Dynamics as a systems/computer programmer. Despite General Dynamics providing a list of housing, she was repeatedly turned away with 'we don't rent to Negroes' once landlords saw her in person. Brown threatened one apartment manager that he would bring a hundred Black friends in bathing suits to swim in the pool; the manager promptly relented and rented her the apartment. Brown describes covenants barring Black homebuyers and CORE's demonstrations against the California Real Estate Association.

improvements: Note that the wife is unnamed in the transcript and the summary correctly does not invent a name.

chapters.title: CORE Direct Action: Bank of America and SDG&E
chapters.summary: Brown details San Diego CORE's campaigns against Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric (SDG&E) to force them to hire Black workers. As chairman of CORE's Western Region (covering California, Oregon, Washington, and possibly Colorado), he led demonstrations including marches, sit-ins, mail-ins (sending payments under or over balance due as a nuisance tactic), and 'coin-ins' (standing in teller lines counting pennies one at a time while others sat on the floor singing). Brown earned multiple jail sentences for these actions and served his time on weekends so he could continue teaching. He was summoned to Sacramento before the state board of trustees to defend his teaching credential; an attorney from CORE accompanied him and the credential was not revoked. Brown notes the San Diego chapter was approximately two-thirds white, with Black participation limited by fear of reprisals. Brown served on CORE's National Advisory Council and became friends with James L. Farmer Jr. and Floyd McKissick. He also discusses fading-out organizations like the NAACP and Urban League in San Diego at the time, and internal CORE conflicts with infiltration from the peace movement and accusations of communist or 'Trotskyite' affiliations. Brown resigned around 1967; after his departure, conflicts between the Black Panthers and Maulana Karenga's US Organization erupted in San Diego.
related_events: Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) direct action
James L. Farmer Jr. (CORE National Director)

improvements: Could explicitly note that the BPP-US Organization conflict Brown references aligns with the documented 1969 UCLA Campbell Hall shootings and the broader 1969-71 BPP-US conflict, but Brown himself attributes the San Diego dynamic to 'hearsay' since he had departed.

chapters.title: Peace Corps in Lesotho and the King Moshoeshoe Memorial
chapters.summary: Brown was invited to apply for a Peace Corps Deputy Director position and was assigned to Lesotho. Traveling with his wife, they had to transit through apartheid South Africa, staying in a segregated room above the Johannesburg airport designated for Africans. Brown tested apartheid by walking out into the main airport and was paged over the PA system. Upon arrival in Lesotho they were warmly greeted; when later Peace Corps volunteers arrived (mostly white), the Basotho were shocked, having assumed Peace Corps workers would all be Black. Brown describes how Lesotho's King Moshoeshoe II was under house arrest for opposing apartheid, while Prime Minister Chief Leabua Jonathan went along with apartheid. After Martin Luther King Jr's assassination, Brown organized a memorial service at an African Methodist Episcopal Church in Lesotho. King Moshoeshoe II attended the service. The Charge d'Affaires reportedly did not appreciate Brown's frank descriptions of American racism in talks at local schools in Maseru. Brown then returned to the United States in 1968, deciding he could not remain abroad while events unfolded at home.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. (April 4, 1968)
House arrest of King Moshoeshoe II of Lesotho

improvements: Could clarify the political history of Moshoeshoe II's relationship with Leabua Jonathan and the 1966 Lesotho independence context, though Brown's account is what is recoverable from this transcript.

chapters.title: Founding the Afro-American Studies Program at San Diego State
chapters.summary: After leaving Lesotho, Brown worked briefly at Floyd McKissick Enterprises in Harlem, then as a loan officer at Marine Midland Bank on Wall Street. A friend who had been around CORE during Brown's leadership contacted him about returning to San Diego to become assistant to the president at San Diego State, with the assignment of organizing a Black studies program. Brown took the position in September 1971, becoming the university's first Black administrator. He hired faculty members fresh from PhD programs at other universities, established a major and minor in Afro-American Studies, and notably succeeded in getting the university to accept the program's courses for general education credit — what Brown calls a 'major coup.' He stayed two years before moving to become associate dean and later director of computer services. The program later became the Africana Studies department. In 1980 Brown became associate dean in the College of Business Administration, retiring in 2004 after 26 years. He founded the Black Economic Development Task Force in 1987 and ran it for 10 years, and founded a certificate program in community economic development.
related_events: Black Studies movement in higher education (late 1960s-1970s)

chapters.title: The Unfinished Agenda: Capitalism, Self-Determination, and Wealth Building
chapters.summary: Brown argues the unfinished work of the civil rights movement is economic education and wealth building. He says the movement focused on opening doors, getting jobs, and access to education but did not address how Black Americans should 'fit into capitalism' to survive and prosper. Brown distinguishes himself from socialism, naming Senator Bernie Sanders as a contrast. He describes founding the Black Economic Development Task Force in 1987 explicitly to push this agenda and notes that even Martin Luther King Jr., despite his greatness, was not focused on economics because his training was theology. Brown stresses the importance of capital ('that's why I called it capitalism'), savings, and Black-owned businesses. He recalls hosting Black Economic Summits in the 1980s. Brown also recounts founding CLUB (Community Leaders Undoing Biases), a monthly business-community discussion group on race that he ran for 10 years before retiring. He praises Tony Brown's KPBS program on Black economics and laments the lack of contemporary leadership on the issue, naming Reverend Jesse L. Jackson Sr., Reverend Al Sharpton, and Representative John Lewis as leaders he respects but who have not centered economics.

improvements: Could clarify that Brown names 'Tony Brown' (the KPBS program host) which is a different person from Harold K. Brown himself, to avoid confusion.

chapters.title: Activist Trauma and the Need for Forced Education
chapters.summary: Brown reflects on how leaving CORE left his 'nervous system shot' and describes what he later recognized as post-traumatic stress, comparing it to what soldiers experience returning from war. He notes other activists who could not deal with the trauma and ended up addicted to drugs. Brown observes that even in his San Diego age cohort, people do not know there was a civil rights movement in San Diego, and that younger people have no idea. He recalls speaking to a class at San Diego State in approximately 1972 about the civil rights movement and finding the students knew nothing. He argues Black history and economic education should be 'force fed' through the schools, originally hoping the Afro-American Studies program he founded would be marketed to the entire university. The transcript closes with brief banter about contemporary politics (Brown jokes about Hillary Rodham Clinton fundraising emails) and the Library of Congress / Smithsonian closing credit.
related_events: Civil rights movement memory and historiography

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Civil Rights Activist; San Diego CORE Chairman; Founder of San Diego State Afro-American Studies Program
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Virginia Tech History Department / Civil Rights History Project

evidence: Chronological arc from York PA childhood through SDSU founding to retirement reflection (Segment 10, 00:01:12,000)
Clear thematic resolution returning to economic self-determination at close (Segment 1075, 01:54:13,580)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Brown moves through a chronological life story with thematic returns to formative racial experiences and the unfinished economic-development agenda. The interviewer's prompts maintain coherence; transitions between sections are mostly smooth though some later sections (Lesotho, CLUB) compress quickly.

most_vivid_moment.quote: I'm going to round up a hundred of my black friends and we're coming over to your apartment building and we're going to take off our clothes. We'll have our bathing suit songs and we're all going to get in that pool.

evidence: Chain factory workers hurling insults at six-year-old Brown walking to school (Segment 32, 00:05:17,000)
Counting pennies one at a time at Bank of America teller windows (Segment 576, 01:06:26,160)
King Moshoeshoe II walking into the AME church memorial for MLK (Segment 849, 01:32:58,280)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Brown delivers several vivid scenes — the pool-threat negotiation, the coin-in tactic, the ululating greeting for King Moshoeshoe II, and the Johannesburg airport apartheid encounter. Many years and place names are precise; some are approximate ('about 1961', 'around 6%').

most_authentic_moment.quote: I left core, I mean my nervous system was shot. I couldn't sit at a meeting or talk to people about it. I couldn't even, my nervous system was completely shot. I didn't realize what was going on but that was close to a nervous breakdown.

vulnerability_elements: Admission of post-traumatic stress after CORE leadership
Frank discussion of CORE internal factionalism being more painful than external segregation
Reflection that he failed to delegate adequately
emotional_authenticity.justification: Brown is unusually candid about the psychological toll of organizing, including a near-breakdown after CORE — a vulnerability rarely articulated this directly in oral histories. He laughs at painful childhood memories and is openly weary about contemporary leadership gaps.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Brown's narrative is well-structured chronologically with strong thematic returns to racial formation, organizational leadership, and economic self-determination. He delivers many vivid set pieces (the pool threat, the coin-in, the Lesotho memorial), and is unusually candid about activist trauma. Some passages compress quickly when he moves through later career milestones.

major_events: Founding and leadership of San Diego CORE chapter circa 1960-1967 (Segment 337, 00:50:24,000)
Bank of America and SDG&E direct action campaigns (Segment 532, 01:02:00,800)
Peace Corps Lesotho deputy directorship and MLK memorial with King Moshoeshoe II (Segment 803, 01:29:03,600)
Founding of San Diego State Afro-American Studies program 1971-1973 (Segment 894, 01:37:11,000)

key_figures.name: James L. Farmer Jr.
key_figures.relationship: CORE National Advisory Council colleague / personal friend

key_figures.name: Floyd McKissick
key_figures.relationship: CORE National Advisory Council colleague; later employer at McKissick Enterprises in Harlem

key_figures.name: King Moshoeshoe II of Lesotho
key_figures.relationship: Attended Brown's MLK memorial service; hosted Brown and wife at palace

organizational_involvement.organization: Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) — San Diego chapter
Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) — Western Region
San Diego State University Afro-American Studies
Black Economic Development Task Force, San Diego

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: West Coast / San Diego CORE chapter leadership perspective on direct action against major California corporations; African American Peace Corps administrator perspective on apartheid-era Lesotho; first Black administrator at San Diego State and founder of its Black studies program.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Brown was a regional CORE leader rather than a national figure, but his firsthand testimony fills documentation gaps about West Coast civil rights organizing, an under-recorded geography. His Peace Corps experience and university institution-building are also distinctive.

precision_examples: Birth year 1934 in York, Pennsylvania, mother Emma Brown, seven siblings (Segment 13, 00:01:35,000)
Army service 1954-1956 at Camp Gordon then Fort Monmouth (Segment 382, 00:55:53,280)
San Diego State approx. 25 Black students of 7,000 enrollment (Segment 263, 00:36:40,040)
September 1971 start at San Diego State (Segment 894, 01:37:11,000)
1987 founding of Black Economic Development Task Force (Segment 943, 01:42:31,280)
verifiable_claims: San Diego State Afro-American Studies founding 1971
Peace Corps Lesotho deputy director appointment
CORE Western Region jurisdiction including California, Oregon, Washington
King Moshoeshoe II's house arrest under Prime Minister Leabua Jonathan
specificity_of_details.justification: Brown provides specific years and full names for most key figures, organizations, and biographical milestones. Some passages use approximate ranges ('around 1960', 'approximately 25 Black students', 'about 6%'), but the level of verifiable detail is solid for a memoir-style interview six decades after events.

new_information: Detailed San Diego CORE chapter organizing history including coin-in tactics at Bank of America
Internal CORE factionalism including peace movement infiltration and accusations of Trotskyite affiliation
African American Peace Corps administrator's apartheid encounter at Johannesburg airport
King Moshoeshoe II attending an MLK memorial during his house arrest
Founding history of San Diego State's Afro-American Studies program
understudied_aspects: West Coast (specifically San Diego) civil rights movement
Black participation in Peace Corps administration in southern Africa
Internal organizational politics during the 1960s civil rights organizations
Black studies institution-building at predominantly white western universities
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Brown complicates the standard South-focused civil rights narrative by documenting sustained direct action and severe housing/employment discrimination in supposedly liberal California. He also complicates the heroic-leadership narrative by openly discussing activist trauma and post-CORE breakdown.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of West Coast civil rights, of CORE's regional chapters, and of post-1965 Black studies institution-building would find primary-source material here. Historians of African diasporic political solidarity during apartheid could use the Moshoeshoe II memorial-service account.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Significant new contributions to the under-documented West Coast civil rights movement, regional CORE history, and Black studies institution-building. The Lesotho material adds a transnational dimension not commonly captured in this corpus.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Brown's interview makes substantial contributions to under-documented histories: West Coast civil rights organizing, regional CORE leadership, African American Peace Corps service in apartheid-adjacent southern Africa, and Black studies institutionalization at predominantly white universities. His proximity to national CORE leadership (Farmer, McKissick) and personal connection to King Moshoeshoe II offer texture historians can use.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Brown traces his evolution from 'a mild, matter-of-fact, quiet, shy kid' interested in athletics to a militant civil rights leader willing to risk his career and freedom, and ultimately to a reflective elder organizing economic-development institutions and reckoning with his own activist trauma.
key_turning_points: Daily racial insults at age six walking to elementary school (Segment 32, 00:05:17,000)
Cassandra Shea introducing him to college as a junior high student (Segment 49, 00:09:42,000)
Refusal to approve discriminatory fraternity charters at San Diego State (Segment 247, 00:34:54,640)
Approach to form San Diego CORE around 1960 (Segment 334, 00:50:08,800)
MLK assassination prompting return from Lesotho (Segment 827, 01:30:57,160)
Founding of Black Economic Development Task Force in 1987 (Segment 943, 01:42:31,280)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Shy athletic Black kid in segregated Pennsylvania to chairman of San Diego CORE and Western Region; loan officer at Marine Midland Bank to first Black administrator at San Diego State University; activist with shot nerves to reflective real-estate developer arguing for Black wealth building.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Brown's articulation of the cost as well as the calling of civil rights work — including the post-CORE trauma — offers a more honest model than purely heroic accounts. His pivot to economic-development institution-building as a third-act vocation is itself instructive.
transformation_growth.justification: Clear development arc with multiple identified turning points, explicit before/after contrasts, and frank reflection on what the work cost personally. Brown's framing of his own transformation as 'How do I get to this point where I'm willing to sacrifice my life and my career' is unusually self-aware.

peak_moments.description: Confronting the racist apartment manager with the threat to bring 100 Black friends to swim in the pool
peak_moments.stakes: Whether his future wife could find housing in San Diego

peak_moments.description: Summoned to Sacramento to defend his teaching credential against revocation due to CORE leadership
peak_moments.stakes: His career as a teacher and his ability to continue organizing

peak_moments.description: Confronting apartheid by walking through Johannesburg airport as an African American
peak_moments.stakes: Personal safety in an apartheid state; risk of detention or worse

peak_moments.description: King Moshoeshoe II walking into the MLK memorial during his house arrest
peak_moments.stakes: Public symbolism of African royalty defying apartheid-aligned government to honor MLK

tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From childhood fear and confusion through organizing-era anger and determination to elder weariness and unresolved grief over the unfinished agenda.
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Multiple high-stakes dramatic moments including direct confrontations with both segregationists and state authorities. The conflicts are personal as well as political — the internal CORE struggle is described as more painful than external segregation, which is an unusually candid framing.

universal_themes: Mentor influence (the homeroom teacher who said 'go to college')
Generational continuity (the gap between his cohort and younger Black activists)
relatable_struggles: Trying to find a job for which one is qualified and being told no
Trying to find a place to live and being turned away
The cost of standing up for what one believes
Recognizing one's own trauma decades after the fact
human_moments: Brown joking that he didn't know he was Black until his mother took him home from the hospital (Segment 36, 00:06:05,000)
Brown's wife complaining to the Y cleaning lady who told her 'you call Hal Brown' (Segment 480, 00:57:09,840)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Audiences who have never organized for civil rights can still relate to housing rejection, professional gatekeeping, mentor influence, and the long-term cost of sustained advocacy work.
relatable_experiences.justification: Brown's narrative threads universal themes (mentorship, partnership, burnout) through the specifically civil rights context. The pool-threat anecdote and the Y-cleaning-lady recommendation are humanizing without being trivializing.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Strong transformation arc with clear personal stakes and several dramatic high-water moments. Brown's openness about post-organizing trauma is unusual and resonant; his framing of the unfinished economic agenda gives the interview forward-leaning emotional weight rather than nostalgic closure.

barriers: Brown uses some period-specific terminology ('Negro', 'colored') that he contextualizes by era
Occasional unexplained acronyms (SDG&E, AME) but most are clarified by context
language_clarity.justification: Brown is articulate and clear throughout. He explains organizational acronyms when introducing them, contextualizes period-specific language, and his San Diego academic background shows in a measured, accessible delivery.

slow_sections: Mid-section navigation of various dean positions at San Diego State (Segments 928-940)
Closing dialogue about Hillary Clinton fundraising emails (Segments 1365-1376)
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: A two-hour-twenty-minute interview is long, but Brown's mix of specific dramatic anecdotes and reflective commentary should sustain attention for audiences interested in the period.
narrative_flow.justification: David Cline asks well-paced, open questions that give Brown room to develop scenes. Pacing dips in the post-CORE career sequence and the closing banter, but the major narrative arcs maintain momentum.

themes_connected: Northern segregation challenges the South-centric civil rights narrative
Economic self-determination as the unfinished civil rights agenda
Black studies in higher education as movement institutionalization
Apartheid and African American transnational solidarity
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Brown explicitly connects to contemporary issues — naming Black Lives Matter, Bernie Sanders, Hillary Clinton, and the lack of present-day leadership on Black economic development. He frames his oral history within the project's larger goal of 'history right now.'
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Brown is explicit about historical meaning: he discusses what should have been done, what was done, and what still needs to be done. The closing reflections turn the interview into an argument as well as a memoir.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Brown consistently links personal narrative to broader civil rights themes and to contemporary debates, making the 'so what' explicit rather than leaving it for the audience to extract.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Highly accessible interview from an articulate narrator who self-contextualizes period language and consistently connects personal stories to broader themes. Interviewer pacing is strong; the length is the main accessibility constraint.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: Harold K. Brown's interview is a substantive contribution to the under-documented West Coast civil rights movement, regional CORE leadership history, African American Peace Corps service in apartheid-era southern Africa, and Black studies institutionalization at predominantly white universities. He is articulate, candid about both organizational successes and personal costs, and grounds his testimony with specific names, dates, and tactics. The interview's main limitations are pacing dips in the post-CORE career navigation and a closing section that meanders into contemporary political asides. Brown's frank discussion of post-organizing trauma and his sustained argument for economic self-determination as the unfinished civil rights agenda give the interview both human depth and forward-leaning analytical weight.
key_strengths: Detailed firsthand testimony on under-documented San Diego CORE direct-action campaigns (Bank of America, SDG&E)
Unusual candor about post-organizing trauma and activist burnout, with explicit comparison to PTSD
Sustained argument that economic self-determination is the unfinished civil rights agenda, grounded in his Black Economic Development Task Force work
key_limitations: Some career milestones and dates after the CORE era are compressed or approximated, limiting precision for biographers
The Lesotho material is rich but brief; some context about King Moshoeshoe II's political situation is left implicit

claims.claim: Harold K. Brown, known as Hal Brown, was born in 1934 in York, Pennsylvania
claims.supporting_excerpt: My name is Harold K. Brown known, better known as Hal Brown. I was born in York, Pennsylvania, and migrated to San Diego through San Diego State University in 1953. ... I was born in 1934, and I am the son of Mrs. Emma Brown, and I am one of seven siblings.
claims.rationale: Brown states this directly in segments 10-13 of the transcript.

claims.claim: Brown was one of seven siblings of Mrs. Emma Brown
claims.supporting_excerpt: I am the son of Mrs. Emma Brown, and I am one of seven siblings.

claims.claim: Brown attended segregated schools through seventh grade and walked approximately five miles past white schools
claims.supporting_excerpt: We lived there, we went to segregated schools up until the seventh grade is when we integrated. ... when we would walk all somewhere around maybe five miles to the school, pass white schools to get there.

claims.claim: Workers at a chain factory hurled racial insults at Brown beginning at age six
claims.supporting_excerpt: And there was a chain works factory on en route to our school. And the men used to hang out the windows on the upper floors and just hurl all kinds of racial insults, calling us names all the whole time. ... And so that was my introduction at age six.

claims.claim: His junior high homeroom teacher Cassandra Shea, a white math teacher, introduced him to the concept of college
claims.supporting_excerpt: My home-room teacher, Cassandra Shea, just sort of adopted me and she was a white math teacher and my home-room teacher. But she introduced me the concept of college, which I didn't know anything about, never heard of.
claims.rationale: Direct quote from segment 49. The name 'Cassandra Shea' is as rendered by Whisper; it may be a phonetic approximation of the actual teacher's name but is treated as supported given the transcript's own rendering.

claims.claim: Brown was all-state basketball player in his senior year, following his brother who was the first basketball player in the school's history to achieve that status
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was selected as the all-state basketball player in my senior year and followed in my brother's foot steps because he had also achieved that as the first basketball player in the history of our school to achieve that status of all-state status. And I was a second.

claims.claim: Brown attended Penn State on a basketball scholarship that paid only tuition
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I attended Penn State on a scholarship, on a basketball scholarship, which paid for my tuition, but that was about it.

claims.claim: Brown tried out with the St. Louis Browns in Thomasville, Georgia, was injured, and did not play
claims.supporting_excerpt: So I eventually left there and went to accept a trial with the St. Louis Browns in Thomasville, Georgia. ... So I was playing. I was a shortstop and I was trying to throw from short to first and needles and things just felt like all through my arms. So that was a bad try.

claims.claim: Brown served in the Army from 1954 to 1956, with basic training at Camp Gordon in Georgia and permanent station at Fort Monmouth, New Jersey
claims.supporting_excerpt: I went in in 54 and I was separated from the army in 56. ... It was with the Camp Gordon. ... Then I was then sent to New Jersey at Fort Momoth where that was my permanent station there.

claims.claim: Brown was assigned to Operation Sagebrush in Louisiana and went to the post commander to ask not to be sent
claims.supporting_excerpt: I received an assignment to go to Louisiana on an operation called Operation Sagebrush. ... So in my naivete decided I was going to the post commander and tell him I'm not going down there.

claims.claim: Brown's major at San Diego State was physical education with a minor in speech pathology / speech correction
claims.supporting_excerpt: My major was in physical education and my minor was in speech correction or the speech pathology area.

claims.claim: Brown chaired the Constitutions Committee in student government and refused to approve fraternity charters specifying 'white male' or 'white female' membership
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was asked to chair a certain committee. It was called the Constitution's Committee. ... So when I started reviewing these I saw in the charters of the fraternities, of white fraternities and sororities that it was for white male or white female and in good conscience I couldn't approve that.

claims.claim: Brown estimated approximately 25 Black students at San Diego State in an enrollment of around 7,000
claims.supporting_excerpt: I think there were about 25 black students in the whole university at that time and out of an enrollment I think it must have been around the year 7000 something like that.

claims.claim: Brown was told by La Mesa, California that the only way he could be hired was if the district polled all teachers about hiring a Negro
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I think this guy was the superintendent. Said the only way I could hire me is if they pulled all the teachers in the district to see if it would be okay to hire a Negro.

claims.claim: Brown joined the El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee, an all-white group
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was approached by an organization that was starting called the El Cajon Valley Open Housing Committee. And they formed to, for the purpose of integrating the neighborhoods in La Mesa, California and El Cajon Valley, just east of San Diego State. And so I joined with them. This was an all white group.

claims.claim: Brown joined San Diego CORE around 1960 and became chairman around 1961
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then I did. And I think that was 1960. And so that started my career in core in that year. ... And then I decided, OK, I'll go and then I became the chairman of the San Diego Corps. ... So this is around 1960. Correct. That was about 61. 61.
claims.rationale: Brown gives 1960 as the joining year (segment 337) and 1961 as the chairmanship year (segments 375-378).

claims.claim: Brown joined the Afro-American Association, described as a Black nationalist organization focused on teaching Black history
claims.supporting_excerpt: I had also become a member of an organization which was known as the Black National Disorganization. It was the Afro-American Association. Now, it was labeled, I think, a Black nationalist organization. ... So the purpose of that organization was still there's a need today. We formed it to educate Black people about the plight of Black people in America, teaching Black history and having discussions and so forth about the conditions of Black folk in America.
claims.rationale: Direct quote from segments 405-413. Note Whisper rendered 'Black Nationalist Organization' as 'Black National Disorganization' which is clearly a transcription error; the canonical name is preserved.

claims.claim: Brown read Lerone Bennett Jr's work through the Afro-American Association
claims.supporting_excerpt: I reached a point where I remember, I think the first one I read was Lerone Bennett Jr Jr by Lerone Bennett Jr and I had discussions with young people about our history.
claims.rationale: Direct quote from segment 421. Brown names Lerone Bennett Jr as the first author he read.

claims.claim: There were 19 segregated schools in San Diego according to Brown
claims.supporting_excerpt: Not only were there segregated schools, but there's I recall there were 19 schools in San Diego that were segregated.

claims.claim: Black workers could not be hired at grocery store chains, even as baggers
claims.supporting_excerpt: Now blacks could not be hired in the grocery stores. These chain stores and everything. They didn't hire blacks even as ... baggers. Yes, much less than other positions of cashier and imaginary. You couldn't even be hired as a bagger.

claims.claim: Brown received jail sentences from Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric demonstrations
claims.supporting_excerpt: And our biggest project was against the Bank of America, which that's where I received my jail sentences from the Bank of America demonstrations and also from the San Diego Gas and Electric demonstrations.

claims.claim: Brown was chairman of CORE's Western Region covering California, Oregon, Washington, and possibly Colorado
claims.supporting_excerpt: So at that time I was chairman of the Western Region of CORE. ... All the states from I think we even included Colorado, but in California, in Oregon, in Washington.

claims.claim: Brown served on CORE's National Advisory Council and became friends with James L. Farmer Jr. and Floyd McKissick
claims.supporting_excerpt: Yeah, I was on the advisory council, National Advisory Council. ... That's where James L. Farmer Jr and I became friends in Floyd, McKissick, and I became friends.
claims.rationale: Direct quote from segments 545-546. The names James L. Farmer Jr and Floyd McKissick are canonical and match the ground-truth corpus.

claims.claim: CORE used coin-in tactics involving counting pennies at teller windows while others sat singing
claims.supporting_excerpt: The coin in was we would stand in line at the Tellers window. We would have a lot of pennies with us and we would either ask for pennies. We would give them $10 and we would ask for some change. We would stand there and count all the pennies. We would bring pennies with us. We got to the front of the window. We would make our deposit or whatever we would do and we count out pennies one at a time one at a time while the others were sitting on the floor singing our songs and things.

claims.claim: Brown was summoned to Sacramento to defend his teaching credential, with an attorney from CORE, and the credential was not revoked
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was called to appear in Sacramento at the board of trustees who were over the education state superintendent and so forth. I was called to come there to explain why I should not have my teaching credential removed because of my leadership in Corps. I had a lawyer who was in Corps as well and he went with me up to go to defend myself. ... but they didn't take my credential.

claims.claim: San Diego CORE was approximately two-thirds white
claims.supporting_excerpt: There were blacks and whites and the whites were probably two-thirds of the chapter.

claims.claim: Brown married his wife in 1967; she was recruited from Columbus, Ohio by General Dynamics as a math major from Alabama State University to be a computer/systems programmer
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then I talk about an example of that is when the young lady that I married in 1967, she came to San Diego. She was recruited to San Diego. ... She was as a math major who graduated from Alabama State University. She came, she was living in Ohio, Columbus, Ohio at the time. She was recruited to come to San Diego to work for general dynamics as a systems programmer, computer programmer.

claims.claim: Brown threatened an apartment manager that he would bring 100 Black friends in bathing suits to swim in the pool
claims.supporting_excerpt: I'm going to round up a hundred of my black friends and we're coming over to your apartment building and we're going to take off our clothes. We'll have our bathing suit songs and we're all going to get in that pool.

claims.claim: Brown resigned from CORE around 1967 and joined the Peace Corps as Deputy Director in Lesotho
claims.supporting_excerpt: I think it was 67. ... I was invited into the Peace Corps to apply for a position as deputy director, which I did. And then was assigned to the South Africa, Southern Africa. ... So then you were in Lesotho for a couple of years? No, I was there for one year, just a little under a year.

claims.claim: Brown and his wife had to transit through Johannesburg under apartheid; they were placed in a segregated room for Africans
claims.supporting_excerpt: So when my wife and I went over to Lesotho, we had to stop in Johannesburg. ... So what they did was they had some room that was above the airport somewhere. And that was for blacks. That put you had to stay in that room. You couldn't be in the airport anywhere.

claims.claim: Lesotho King Moshoeshoe II was under house arrest for opposing apartheid, while Prime Minister Chief Leabua Jonathan went along with apartheid
claims.supporting_excerpt: And at the whole time we were there and before we got there, King Moshoeshoe II of Lesotho was under house arrest because he opposed apartheid. And the people were able to put in a Prime Minister Chief Leabua Jonathan who, whether he agreed with the partied and not he went along with the partied.

claims.claim: Brown organized an MLK memorial service at an African Methodist Episcopal Church in Lesotho that King Moshoeshoe II attended
claims.supporting_excerpt: When Martin Luther King was killed, I went to the church to the AME church, African Methodist Episcopal Church there in Lesotho. And I asked we could have a memorial service there for Martin. ... So when we had this church memorial service who walks in the king almost brings tears to my eyes now. He walked in and set in through the whole service.

claims.claim: Brown worked briefly at Floyd McKissick Enterprises in Harlem and at Marine Midland Bank on Wall Street after returning from Lesotho
claims.supporting_excerpt: So that's where in the Florida, McKissick, and I started this company in Harlem, Florida McKissick Enterprises. ... And then I moved to the, to the bank. And I worked for Marine Midland Bank. ... Marine Midland, and my DLA, and the home wall street.
claims.rationale: Direct quote from segments 867-873. Note Whisper rendered 'Floyd' as 'Florida' at one point; the canonical name is preserved as it appears clearly elsewhere in the transcript.

claims.claim: Brown began at San Diego State in September 1971 as the university's first Black administrator, organizing the Afro-American Studies program
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then that's when I started working in September of 1971 at San Diego State. ... Yeah, I was the first black administrator that the university had ever had. ... And then we've established a curriculum. We established a major and a minor in back in that.

claims.claim: Brown succeeded in getting San Diego State to accept Afro-American Studies courses for general education credit
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I was able to get the university to accept our courses to fulfill the general education requirements just like all the others. I mean, that was a major coup.

claims.claim: The Afro-American Studies program later became the Africana Studies department at San Diego State
claims.supporting_excerpt: They changed the name to Africana Studies, rather than African American Studies. But it's a full fledged department in San Diego State.

claims.claim: Brown became associate dean in the College of Business Administration in 1980 and retired in 2004 after 26 years
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then in 1980, I went to become the associate dean in the College of Business Administration and that's where I retired from there after, I guess, 26 years as the associate dean there.
claims.rationale: Direct quote from segment 935-936. Note: he says 1980 to retirement 2004 spans 24 years, not 26; the summary preserves Brown's own '26 years' wording since it is his attribution. Treating as supported with the understanding that the figure is Brown's own.

claims.claim: Brown founded the Black Economic Development Task Force in 1987 and ran it for 10 years
claims.supporting_excerpt: But I'm just reminding myself before that, what I did was, in 1987, I spearheaded the formation of an organization called the Black Economic Development. ... And it was called the Black Economic Development Task Force. And I did that for 10 years.

claims.claim: Brown founded a group called CLUB (Community Leaders Undoing Biases) that met monthly at a hotel
claims.supporting_excerpt: Yeah, it was called, it was called, Club was the acronym Community Leaders Undoing Biasis.
claims.rationale: Direct quote from segment 1008. Whisper rendering 'Biasis' is a transcription error for 'Biases'; the canonical spelling is preserved per the firewall rule treating transcription errors as matches when context confirms.

claims.claim: Brown explicitly contrasts his economic approach with Senator Bernie Sanders
claims.supporting_excerpt: You're not going to replace capitalism as Senator Bernie Sanders might think he can do but we'd like to do it.

claims.claim: Brown praises Tony Brown's KPBS program on Black economics
claims.supporting_excerpt: Tony, Tony Brown? You know about Tony Brown? Tony Brown had a program on KPBS for some years. And his focus was on economics, black businesses and all this stuff.

claims.claim: Brown names Reverend Jesse L. Jackson Sr., Reverend Al Sharpton, and Representative John Lewis as leaders he respects who have not centered economics
claims.supporting_excerpt: And Reverend Jesse L. Jackson Sr disappeared and then we have Reverend Al Sharpton at him. But I remember Jesse used to come out here in San Diego many, many years ago. He would be talking and talking and talking about him and treating all this stuff. But never, nothing, not a word about the importance of economics.
claims.rationale: Direct quote from segments 1359-1362. John Lewis is also mentioned positively earlier (segment 1352: 'if we had somebody like on the level of Representative John Lewis in Congress').

claims.claim: Brown describes post-CORE psychological collapse, comparing it to PTSD
claims.supporting_excerpt: When I finished the Civil Rights Movement ... My left core, I mean my nervous system was shot. I couldn't sit at a meeting or talk to people about it. I couldn't even, my nervous system was completely shot. I didn't realize what was going on but that was close to a nervous breakdown.
claims.rationale: Direct quote from segments 1302-1307, with explicit PTSD comparison in segment 1320: 'that's what they're talking about, about people coming back from the wars and stuff, post-traumatic stress.'

claims.claim: After Brown left CORE, conflicts between the Black Panthers and US Organization erupted in San Diego
claims.supporting_excerpt: my understanding was that the black power movement, the black panther movement and the ... US Organization. You remember the US Organization, there was a problem in San Diego. And I was gone, so all of this is hearsay, but there was a lot of fighting between those two organizations and so on.
claims.rationale: Brown himself flags this as 'hearsay' since he was in Lesotho at the time. The summary preserves Brown's framing but the underlying historical claim about BPP-US conflict in San Diego is documented in the corpus (Maulana Karenga / US Organization entry). Marked partially_supported because Brown's testimony is acknowledged secondhand.

claims.claim: The interview was conducted by David Cline on June 28, 2016 in San Diego, California for the Civil Rights History Project
claims.supporting_excerpt: Like good afternoon, this is David Cline for the Civil Rights History Project. I work in the History Department at Virginia Tech and have the honor of working with the Civil Rights History Project of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture. Today is June 28, 2016, and I'm in San Diego, California.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary's quality_metrics.accuracy_score (84) and quality_score (86) both fall below the 90/90 publication threshold. The accuracy gap reflects honest paraphrase compression risk on several biographical milestones (post-CORE career dates, Lesotho timing, wife's recruitment-arrival sequence) rather than fabricated facts — but a publication-grade summary should not have those compressions unflagged. Additionally, the citation_audit logs 1 partially_supported claim (the BPP-US Organization San Diego conflict, which Brown himself frames as hearsay since he was in Lesotho when it occurred); the summary preserves Brown's hedge but the partial-support status routes to human review per the fail-closed gate. Per the gate logic, both conditions independently require human review. The reviewer should determine whether to tighten the summary's date attributions, add explicit 'Brown recalls' framing for the hearsay claim, and re-score, or to accept Brown's own framings as faithful representation given that he himself flags uncertainty in several places.
